original_hypothesis.title: Disabling Sellable Status Does Not Pause Active Listings
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The setSellable(false) function is intended to halt trading for a specific vesting plan. However, spotPurchase does not check the sellable status, meaning existing listings remain fully active and purchasable.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If a token is compromised or an emergency halt is required, the admin will call setSellable(false). Because this only prevents new listings, attackers can still buy existing active listings, bypassing the intended emergency pause mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: spotPurchase calls _validatePurchase which only checks listing.status == Status.LIST, ignoring vestingSettings[plan].sellable

preconditions: Listings exist for a vesting plan
Admin calls setSellable(false) to halt trading

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker buys tokens that the protocol intended to freeze
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker acquires tokens during an emergency event at stale prices

economic_questions: Can sellers unlist their tokens if sellable is false?
Does the global marketplace freeze work instead?
validation_requirements: Verify spotPurchase execution when sellable == false
Check if global freeze is the only effective pause mechanism

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right after an emergency halt is attempted via setSellable
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Defeats protocol's granular risk management controls

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Sellers who thought their listings were paused
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of tokens at stale prices
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All active listings for the halted plan

validation_result.impact_summary: Setting setSellable(false) only prevents new listings but does not prevent purchases on existing active listings, defeating the per-plan trading halt mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that the `sellable` flag in VestingManager is only checked during `listVesting` (new listing creation) but never during `spotPurchase` (buying from existing listings). When an admin or vestingDeployer calls `setSellable(vesting, false)` intending to halt trading for a specific vesting plan, all existing active listings remain fully purchasable. The `_validatePurchase` function only checks `listing.status == Status.LIST`, whitelist, amounts, and listing type — it does not query `vestingSettings[plan].sellable`. Similarly, `completePurchase` in VestingManager performs no sellable check. This means the per-plan emergency halt mechanism is incomplete: it can prevent new listings but cannot stop execution of existing ones.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof-of-signal is accurate. Lines in spotPurchase call _validatePurchase which checks listing.status, whitelist, amounts — but never vestingSettings[plan].sellable. The listVesting function in VestingManager (line ~134) explicitly requires `vestingSettings[plan].sellable`, confirming the intent for sellable to gate trading. But spotPurchase and completePurchase lack this check entirely.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No check for vestingSettings[vestingPlan].sellable. When sellable is set to false, this function still allows purchases on existing active listings.
completePurchase does not check vestingSettings[vesting].sellable before completing the transfer, allowing purchases to finalize even when the plan's trading is supposed to be halted.
The sellable check is present here for new listings, confirming the protocol intends sellable to control trading. But the analogous check is missing from the purchase flow.

calling_sequence: 1. Admin calls setSellable(vestingPlan, false) on VestingManager — sets vestingSettings[vestingPlan].sellable = false
2. Buyer calls spotPurchase(vestingPlan, listingId, amount, referral) on Marketplace
4. _validatePurchase checks listing.status == LIST, whitelist, amounts — NO sellable check
9. VestingManager.completePurchase transfers vesting to buyer — NO sellable check
10. Purchase completes successfully despite sellable == false
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entire spotPurchase flow from entry to completion never reads vestingSettings[plan].sellable. The sellable flag is only enforced in VestingManager.listVesting() which is called during Marketplace.listVesting(). The purchase path (Marketplace.spotPurchase -> Marketplace._validatePurchase -> VestingManager.completePurchase) completely bypasses this check.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this defect. The sellable flag enforcement gap exists regardless of deployment parameters.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) Token issuer deploys vesting plan, users list vestings on marketplace. (2) A vulnerability is discovered in the vesting token or the issuer needs to halt secondary trading. (3) Admin calls setSellable(vestingPlan, false). (4) New listings are prevented, but all existing active listings remain purchasable. (5) An attacker (or any buyer) can still purchase tokens from all active listings, potentially at stale prices during an emergency event. The admin's only alternative is to freeze the entire marketplace (affecting ALL vesting plans) or manually unlist each individual listing (which costs gas and may incur time delays). This is a real defect in the granular per-plan control mechanism.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The per-vesting-plan sellable control is a key risk management feature that allows administrators to selectively halt trading for specific plans without affecting the entire marketplace. Because this control is incomplete (only blocks new listings, not purchases of existing ones), it fails to provide the intended emergency halt capability. During a security incident affecting a specific vesting plan, attackers could purchase from any existing listing before the admin can either freeze the entire marketplace or manually unlist all affected listings. This defeats the granular risk management architecture.
recommended_mitigations: Add a sellable check in spotPurchase or _validatePurchase: require(IVestingManager(vestingManager).vestingSettings(_vestingPlan).sellable, 'SS_Marketplace: vesting plan not sellable')
Alternatively, add the sellable check in VestingManager.completePurchase: require(vestingSettings[vesting].sellable, 'vesting not sellable')
Consider also adding an unlistVesting mechanism that doesn't require the isFreeze check, allowing admin to force-unlist listings for a disabled plan
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full system (marketplace, settings, vesting manager, step vesting). 2. Create a vesting plan and set sellable=true. 3. Have a seller list some vesting tokens. 4. Admin calls setSellable(plan, false). 5. Verify listVesting reverts for new listings. 6. Call spotPurchase on the existing listing — expect it to succeed, confirming the defect.
uncertainties: The global marketplace freeze (setMarketplaceStatus) could serve as an alternative emergency mechanism, but it affects ALL plans, not just the compromised one
The admin could manually unlist each affected listing, but this requires iterating all listing IDs and is not atomic
additional_testing_needed: Verify that unlistVesting can be called when sellable is false (it currently has isFreeze but no sellable check, so it should work)
Test the scenario where sellers can still unlist their tokens after sellable is set to false
Confirm there's no secondary check in the vesting transfer chain that would prevent completion
